## STOCHASTIC INVERSE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The goal of the inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) problem is to recover the reward functions from expert demonstrations. However, the IRL problem like any ill-posed inverse problem suffers the congenital defect that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions, and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions. We adopt the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) method to estimate the parameter of the probability distribution as the first solution to the SIRL problem. The solution is _succinct, robust, and transferable_ for a learning task and can generate alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Through our formulation, it is possible to observe the intrinsic property for the IRL problem from a _global_ viewpoint, and our approach achieves a considerable performance on the _objectworld_ .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The IRL problem addresses an inverse problem that a set of expert demonstrations determines a reward function over a Markov decision process (MDP) if the model dynamics are known Russell (1998); Ng et al. (2000). The recovered reward function provides a _succinct, robust, and transferable_ definition of the learning task and completely determines the optimal policy. However, the IRL problem is ill-posed that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. For example, all policies are optimal for a constant reward function. In a real-world scenario, experts always act sub-optimally or inconsistently, which is another challenge.

To overcome these limitations, two classes of probabilistic approaches for the IRL problem are proposed, i.e., Bayesian inverse reinforcement learning (BIRL) Ramachandran & Amir (2007) based on Bayesians’ maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation and maximum entropy IRL (MaxEnt) Ziebart et al. (2008); Ziebart (2010) based on frequentists’ maximum likelihood (MLE) estimation. BIRL solves for the distribution of reward functions without an assumption that experts behave optimally, and encode the external _a priori_ information in a choice of a prior distribution. However, BIRL also suffers from the practical limitation that a large number of algorithmic iterations is required for the procedure of Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) in a sampling of posterior over reward functions. Advanced techniques, for example Kernel technique Michini & How (2012) and gradient method Choi & Kim (2011), are proposed to improve the efficiency and tractability of this situation.

MaxEnt employs the principle of maximum entropy to resolve the ambiguity in choosing demonstrations over a policy. This class of methods, inheriting the merits from previous non-probabilistic IRL approaches including Ng et al. (2000); Abbeel & Ng (2004); Ratliff et al. (2006); Abbeel et al. (2008); Syed & Schapire (2008); Ho et al. (2016), imposes regular structures of reward functions in a combination of hand-selected features. Formally, the reward function is a linear or nonlinear combination of the feature basis functions which consists of a set of real-valued functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}i_ =1 hand-selected by experts. The goal of this approach is to find the best-fitting weights of feature basis functions through the MLE approach. Wulfmeier et al. (2015) and Levine et al. (2011) use deep neural networks and Gaussian processes to fit the parameters based on demonstrations respectively but still suffer from the problem that the true reward shaped by the changing environment dynamics.

Influenced by the work of Finn et al. (2016a;b), Fu et al. (2017) propose a framework called adversarial IRL (AIRL) to recover robust reward functions in a changing dynamics based on adversarial learning and achieves superior results. Compared with AIRL, another adversarial method called generative

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) Ho & Ermon (2016) seeks to directly recover the expert’s policy rather than reward functions. Many follow-up methods enhance and extend GAIL for multipurpose in various application scenarios Li et al. (2017); Hausman et al. (2017); Wang et al. (2017). However, GAIL is in a lack of an explanation of expert’s behavior and a portable representation for the knowledge transfer which are the merits of the class of the MaxEnt approach, because the MaxEnt approach is equipped with the "transferable" regular structures over reward functions.

In this paper, under the framework of the MaxEnt approach, we propose a generalized perspective of studying the IRL problem called _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL). It is formulated as an expectation optimization problem aiming to recover a probability distribution over the reward function from expert demonstrations. The solution of SIRL is _succinct_ and _robust_ for the learning task in the meaning that it can generate more than one weight over feature basis functions which compose alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Benefits of the class of the MaxEnt method, the solution to our generalized problem SIRL is also _transferable_ . Since of the intractable integration in our formulation, we employ the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) approach Wei & Tanner (1990) to give the first solution to the SIRL problem in a model-based environment. In general, the solutions to the IRL problem are not always best-fitting in the previous approaches because a highly nonlinear inverse problem with the limited information is very likely to get trapped in a secondary maximum in the recovery. Taking advantage of the Monte Carlo mechanism of a _global_ exhaustive search, our MCEM approach avoids the secondary maximum and theoretically convergent demonstrated by pieces of literature Caffo et al. (2005); Chan & Ledolter (1995). Our approach is also quickly convergent because of the preset simple geometric configuration over weight space in which we approximate it with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). Hence, our approach works well in a real-world scenario with a small and variability set of expert demonstrations.

In particular, the contributions of this paper are threefold:

1. We generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem SIRL.

2. We provide the first theoretically existing solution to SIRL by the MCEM approach.

3. We show the effectiveness of our approach by comparing the performance of the proposed method to those of the previous algorithms on the _objectworld_ .

## 2 PRELIMINARY

An MDP is a tuple _M_ := _⟨S, A, T , R, γ⟩_ , where _S_ is the set of states, _A_ is the set of actions, and the transition function (a.k.a. model dynamics) _T_ := P( _st_ +1 = _s[′]_[��] _st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) for _s, s[′] ∈S_ and _a ∈A_ is the probability of being current state _s_ , taking action _a_ and yielding next state _s[′]_ . Reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) is a real-valued function and _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) is the discount factor. A policy _π_ : _S →A_ is deterministic or stochastic, where the deterministic one is written as _a_ = _π_ ( _s_ ), and the stochastic one is as a conditional distribution _π_ ( _a|s_ ). Sequential decisions are recorded in a series of episodes which consist of states _s_ , actions _a_ , and rewards _r_ . The goal of reinforcement learning aims to get optimal policy _π[∗]_ for maximizing the expected total reward, i.e.

Given an MDP without a reward function _R_ , i.e., MDP _\_ R= _⟨S, A, T ⟩_ , and _m_ expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ := _{ζ_[1] _, · · · , ζ[m] }_ . Each expert demonstration _ζ[i]_ is a sequential of state-action pairs. The goal of the IRL problem is to estimate the unknown reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) from expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ and the reward functions compose a complete MDP. The estimated complete MDP yields an optimal policy that acts as closely as the expert demonstrations.

## 2.2.1 NOTE:

- Problem 1 is well-posed and typically not intractable analytically.

- Trajectory element set _O_ is usually known from the rough estimation of the statistics in expert demonstrations in practice.

- We introduce _representative trajectory class_ to overcome the limitation of the sub-optimality of expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ Ramachandran & Amir (2007), which is also called a lack of sampling representativeness in statistics Kruskal & Mosteller (1979), e.g., driver’s demonstrations encode his own preferences in driving style but may not reflect the true rewards of an environment. With the technique of representative trajectory class, each subset of driver’s demonstrations (i.e. trajectory element set _O_ ) constitutes a subproblem in Problem 1.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate our approach on a classic environment _objectworld_ introduced by Levine et al. (2011) which is a particularly challenging environment with a large number of irrelevant features and the highly nonlinearity of the reward functions. Note that since almost only _objectworld_ provides a tool that allows analysis and display the evolution procedure of the SIRL problem in a 2D heat map, we skip the typical invisible physics-based control tasks for the evaluation of our approach, i.e. cartpole Barto et al. (1983), mountain car Moore (1990), MuJoCo Todorov et al. (2012), and etc.
We employ the expected value difference (EVD) proposed by Levine et al. (2011) to be the metric of optimality as follows:

which is a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy _π[∗]_ , given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ), where Θ _[∗]_ is the best estimation parameter in our approach.

## 4.1 OBJECTWORLD

The _objectworld_ is a learning environment for the IRL problem. It is an _N × N_ grid board with colored objects placed in randomly selected cells. Each colored object is assigned one inner color and one outer color from _C_ preselected colors. Each cell on the grid board is a state, and stepping to four neighbor cells (up, down, left, right) or staying in place (stay) are five actions with a 30% chance of moving in a random direction.

The ground truth of the reward function is defined in the following way. Suppose two primary colors of _C_ preselected colors are red and blue. The reward of a state is 1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object and 2 steps of an outer blue object, -1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object, and 0 otherwise. The other pairs of inner and outer colors are distractors. Continuous and discrete versions of feature basis functions are provided. For the continuous version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a 2 _C_ -dimensional real-valued feature vector. Each dimension records the Euclidean distance from the state to objects. For example, the first and second coordinates are the distances to the nearest inner and outer red object respectively, and so on through all _C_ colors. For the discrete version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a (2 _C · N_ )-dimensional binary feature vector. Each _N_ -dimensional vector records a binary representation of the distance to the nearest inner or outer color object with the _d_ -th coordinate 1 if the corresponding continuous distance is less than _d_ .

Additionally, the architecture of neural networks in DeepMaxEnt and DSIRL are implemented as 3-layer fully-connected with the sigmoid function.

## 4.2.1 EVALUATION PLATFORM

All the methods are implemented in Python 3.5 and Theano 1.0.0 with a machine learning distributed framework _Ray_ Moritz et al. (2018). The experiments are conducted on a machine with Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7700 CPU @ 3.60GHz and Nvidia GeForce GTX 1070 GPU.

## 4.2.2 RECOVERY EXPERIMENT

In the recovery experiment, we compare the true reward function, the optimal policy, and the optimal value with ones derived from expert demonstrations under the four methods. Since our approach is an average of all the outcomes which are prone to imitate the true optimal value from expert demonstrations, we use the mean of reward conditional probability distribution for SIRL and DSIRL as a comparison.

In Figure 1, the EVD of the optimal values in the last row are 48.9, 31.1, 33.7 and 11.3 for four methods respectively, and the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively. It yields that in a highly nonlinear inverse problem, the recovery abilities of SIRL and DISRL are better than MaxEnt’s and DeepMaxEnt’s respectively. The reason is mainly because Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach alleviates the problem of getting stuck in local minima by allowing random exit from it.

## 4.2.4 HYPERPARAMETER EXPERIMENT

In the hyperparameter experiment, we evaluate the effectiveness of our approach under the influence of different preset quantities and qualities of expert demonstrations. The amount of information carried in expert demonstrations composes a specific learning environment, and hence it has an impact on the effectiveness of our generative model. We verify three hyperparameters including the number of expert demonstrations in Figure 3, the trajectory length of expert demonstrations in Figure 4 and the portion size in representative trajectory class _Cϵ[E]_[in Figure][ 5][ on the] _[ objectworld]_[.][The] shadow of the line in the figures represents the standard error for each experimental trail. Notice that the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing as the number and the trajectory length of expert demonstrations, and the portion size in the representative trajectory class are increasing. A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose a generalized problem SIRL for the IRL problem to get the distribution of reward functions. The new problem is well-posed and we employ the method of MCEM to give the first _succinct, robust, and transferable_ solution. In the experiment, we evaluate our approach on the _objectworld_ and the experimental results confirm the effectiveness of our approach.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper introduces "Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning" (SIRL), a framework that reframes IRL as an expectation optimization problem to recover a distribution over reward functions rather than a point estimate. The presentation is academically ambitious and correctly identifies the "ill-posed" nature of standard IRL as a primary motivation for moving toward probabilistic modeling. However, the clarity of the writing is significantly hindered by non-native phrasing, awkward transitions, and a lack of technical precision in key definitions. Notably, the argument that SIRL is "well-posed" compared to existing MaxEnt or Bayesian approaches remains underspecified in the introduction; the manuscript would benefit from a more rigorous explanation of how the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) configuration mathematically resolves the ambiguity where previous methods (like BIRL or MaxEnt) struggle. Furthermore, the reliance on the "Objectworld" benchmark—a relatively simple and dated environment—raises concerns regarding the claims of generalizability and robustness in complex, real-world scenarios. To elevate the manuscript to the standard of a top-tier conference like ICLR, the authors must tighten the technical focus, provide a clearer distinction between their approach and existing MCMC-based Bayesian IRL, and supplement their empirical evaluation with more modern or demanding benchmarks.

comments.explanation: The manuscript contains critical gaps where equations for the primary objective function, stopping criteria ($\delta_{EM}, \epsilon_{EM}$), and Monte Carlo sample size requirements are missing. Without these, the core method and its convergence guarantees are unverifiable and impossible to reproduce.

comments.title: Overstatement of Convergence and Global Optimality
comments.quote: Our MCEM approach avoids the secondary maximum...
comments.explanation: The authors claim their approach 'avoids the secondary maximum.' This is a significant overstatement given that the underlying IRL problem is non-convex. The cited literature provides conditions for convergence to local maxima, not global ones. The claim lacks a formal connection to the objective function's structure.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), which formulates IRL as an expectation optimization problem to recover a distribution over reward functions using Monte Carlo Expectation-Maximization (MCEM) within a Maximum Entropy framework. While the motivation to address the ill-posedness of IRL via distributional recovery is sound and the prose is generally accessible, the paper's quality is significantly compromised by a lack of novelty relative to existing work and questionable theoretical claims. The most critical issue is the failure to clearly distinguish SIRL from Bayesian IRL (BIRL); since BIRL already seeks a posterior over rewards, the proposed method appears to be a re-implementation of BIRL using MCEM instead of MCMC, yet the authors do not adequately justify why MCEM is preferable to scalable BIRL approximations or variational inference, nor do they substantiate the bold claim that MCEM achieves a "global exhaustive search" to avoid secondary maxima, which contradicts standard understanding of EM convergence. Additionally, the manuscript inherits the feature-dependence limitations of MaxEnt approaches, raising doubts about the claimed "robustness" and "transferability" without rigorous validation on diverse or dynamic environments, and the evaluation on the restricted "objectworld" benchmark is insufficient to demonstrate superiority over modern adversarial or deep imitation baselines. The writing requires refinement to eliminate grammatical irregularities and to precisely define the scope of the contribution.

comments.title: Conflation of Profile Parameter Θ₁ and Sampled Reward Weights W
comments.quote: The parameter Θ_1^[t+1] is represented as Θ_1^[t+1] := (W_1^[m]_[Θ^t], · · · , W_N_t^[m]_[Θ^t]).
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by how Θ₁ is defined in Stage 1. The paper introduces a profile parameter decomposition Θ := (Θ₁, Θ₂), where Θ₁ is stated to govern model/trajectory parameters and Θ₂ governs reward distribution parameters. However, the authors then equate Θ₁^[t+1] directly with the collection of Monte Carlo sampled reward weights {W}. Furthermore, they note that Θ₁^[t+1] has N_t coordinates, where N_t is the number of samples at iteration t, meaning the dimensionality of the parameter vector changes every iteration. In a standard EM/MCEM framework, the model parameters (Θ₁) should have fixed dimensionality and be optimized given the latent variables (W), not be identified with the latent variables themselves. This conflation obscures the actual optimization target in Stage 1 and contradicts the stated profile decomposition, making the methodological description ambiguous.

comments.title: Imprecise Terminology for GMM Covariance Bounds
comments.quote: the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the phrase 'covariances ... limited up to X on each coordinate' struck me as mathematically imprecise. A covariance is a full matrix, whereas bounding a scalar value 'per coordinate' typically refers to the diagonal elements (variances) or standard deviations. Given the context of reporting model complexity or spread, the authors likely mean the maximum variance along any dimension or the diagonal norms. Using 'covariance' interchangeably with coordinate-wise variance is imprecise and could mislead readers regarding the actual correlation structure and magnitude of the learned GMM components.

comments.title: Notation Inconsistency in Algorithm 1 Solution Set
comments.quote: **for** _any W[′] ∈S_ **do**
comments.explanation: In the input/output description of Algorithm 1, the output is explicitly defined as 'Solution set _G_ := _{Wi}[N] i_ =1[.]'. However, within the while loop, the membership check uses a different symbol: 'W' ∈ S'. This is a clear notation inconsistency. A careful reader attempting to reproduce the algorithm would be confused about whether S refers to G, a temporary set, or an undefined set. It should be corrected to W' ∈ G for consistency and reproducibility.

comments.title: Typographical Error in Method Comparison (DISRL vs DSIRL)
comments.quote: It yields that in a highly nonlinear inverse problem, the recovery abilities of SIRL and DISRL are better than MaxEnt’s and DeepMaxEnt’s respectively.
comments.explanation: The experiment section evaluates four algorithms: MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, and DSIRL. In the conclusion of the recovery experiment paragraph, 'DSIRL' is typoed as 'DISRL'. While the intent is clear from context and the preceding discussion, this minor error introduces unnecessary confusion when mapping the reported EVD values and covariance bounds to the specific methods being compared.

comments.title: Terminology mix-up regarding EVD and 'optimal value'
comments.quote: The optimal value derived from these recovered reward functions has a very small EVD value with the true reward.
comments.explanation: The authors define EVD in Section 4 and the Notation section as 'a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy \pi^*, given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution'. In reinforcement learning, the 'optimal value' standardly refers to the value function V^*(s), not the expected return of a derived policy. Additionally, EVD quantifies the performance gap between two policies, not a direct relationship with the 'true reward' itself. The sentence conflates the optimal value function with the derived policy's expected return and awkwardly phrases what the metric represents relative to the ground truth. It likely intends to state that the *policy* generated by the recovered rewards achieves an expected return very close to that of the optimal policy, resulting in a small EVD. This is a minor terminology inaccuracy that could confuse readers about the precise mathematical definition of the metric.

comments.title: Grammatical error in pluralization
comments.quote: It yields the effectiveness of our _robust_ generative model which can generate more than one solutions to the IRL problem.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'more than one solutions' contains a clear grammatical error. It should be corrected to 'more than one solution' or 'multiple solutions'. While this does not affect the underlying mathematical claims or methodology, it is a noticeable linguistic flaw that impacts the readability of the conclusion.

### High-Level Feedback

This paper introduces a novel formulation, Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), which recasts the ill-posed IRL problem as a well-posed expectation optimization over a probability distribution of reward functions. The core idea is interesting and timely, and the motivation to address ambiguity and local optima in IRL is well-founded. However, the manuscript suffers from several significant issues that hinder its clarity and impact. First, the exposition is often imprecise and underdeveloped; for instance, the claim that the SIRL solution is "succinct, robust, and transferable" is asserted repeatedly without formal definition or rigorous justification of what these terms mean in the context of a distribution over rewards. Second, the relationship between the proposed MCEM solution and the actual optimization problem is described only vaguely—the paper states that MCEM is adopted but fails to specify the latent variables, the complete-data likelihood, or how the Monte Carlo expectation step is tractably performed, especially given the intractable integration over reward functions. Third, the experimental validation is limited to a single domain (objectworld) and lacks critical comparisons to strong baselines (e.g., MaxEnt, AIRL) in terms of reward recovery quality and robustness to suboptimal demonstrations. Finally, the paper's claim of "theoretically existing solution" is overstated, as no convergence or consistency guarantees are derived for the specific SIRL setting. For a conference paper at ICLR, the work needs substantially more rigorous theoretical grounding, clearer algorithmic details, and more comprehensive empirical evaluation to demonstrate that the stochastic formulation offers a concrete advantage over existing deterministic and Bayesian approaches.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of representative trajectory class C_ε^E
comments.quote: Suppose a representative trajectory class C_ε^E satisfies that each trajectory element set O ∈ C_ε^E is a subset of expert demonstrations ζ^E with the cardinality at least ε · |ζ^E|, where ε is a preset threshold and |ζ^E| is the number of expert demonstrations, written as,
comments.explanation: The sentence introduces C_ε^E but the formal definition is cut off (the text says 'written as,' but the equation is missing). The prose description is somewhat clear, but the missing formal set-builder notation is a gap. Since the paper uses this class in the optimization problem and in the algorithm, the definition is underspecified—e.g., are all subsets of that size included? Is it the set of all subsets of size ≥ ε|ζ^E|, or a specific collection? The ambiguity could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency: joint distribution of O and W in formulation vs. sampling in algorithm
comments.quote: where reward weights at the t-th iteration W_i^{Θ^t} are randomly drawn from the reward conditional probability distribution D^R(W|ζ^E, Θ^t) and compose a set of learning tasks M_i^t := (S, A, T, R_{W_i^{Θ^t}}, γ) with a trajectory element set O_i^t uniformly drawn from representative trajectory class C_ε^E, for i = 1, ..., N_t.
comments.explanation: The formulation in Equation 1 optimizes an expectation over O and W jointly. However, in the MCEM procedure, the algorithm samples O_i^t and W_i^{Θ^t} independently (W from D^R, O uniformly from C_ε^E) as if the joint distribution factorizes. This is consistent if O and W are independent conditional on Θ, but the paper does not state this assumption. Moreover, in the definition of f_M(O, W|Θ) from Equation 2, it is written as a product g_M(O|W, Θ1) h_M(W|Θ2), which implies O depends on W. Yet the algorithm draws O independently of W. There is a logical inconsistency: if O depends on W (as in the factorization), then sampling O uniformly from C_ε^E without conditioning on W contradicts the model.

comments.title: Underspecified gradient ascent step in Stage 1
comments.quote: In practice, we move m steps uphill to the optimum in each learning task M_i^t. The update formula of m-step reward weights W_{m}^{Θ_i^t} is written as
comments.explanation: The text mentions moving 'm steps uphill' with a learning rate λ_i^t, but the update formula is not shown in the extracted passage. The reader is left to guess whether this is gradient ascent on the log posterior or some other objective. The pseudocode in the appendix is referenced, but without it, the method is underspecified. This is a reproducibility gap: the key inner optimization is not described.

comments.title: Intractable expectation claim contradicts 'not intractable analytically'
comments.quote: Problem 1 is well-posed and typically not intractable analytically.
comments.explanation: In the NOTE (Section 2.2.1), the authors claim Problem 1 is 'typically not intractable analytically'. However, later in the Methodology (Section 3), they repeatedly refer to 'the intractable expectation of Equation 4' and 'the intractable expectation of Equation 5' and resort to Monte Carlo approximations. This is a direct contradiction: if the expectation were tractable analytically, there would be no need for MCEM. The statement as written is misleading and undermines the motivation for the proposed method.

comments.title: Missing definition of function g_M in Stage 1 update
comments.quote: (Θ^{t+1}_1)_i := arg max_θ log g_Mt_i (O_i^t | W_i^{Θ^t}, θ)
comments.explanation: The function g_M is introduced in Equation 2 as a factor of the joint density f_M, but its explicit form is never defined anywhere in the provided text. The reader is told it is 'convex formulation maximized by a gradient ascent method', which implies it is likely a log-likelihood of trajectories given weights, but the precise dependency on the MDP is not given. Without knowing g_M, the update rule is incomplete.

comments.title: Conflation of parameter Θ_1 with Monte Carlo samples
comments.quote: Hence, the parameter Θ^{t+1}_1 is represented as Θ^{t+1}_1 := {W^{m,Θ}_1^t, ..., W^{m,Θ}_{N_t}^t}.
comments.explanation: In the first stage, Θ_1^{t+1} is said to have N_t coordinates, each being a weight vector. This means Θ_1^{t+1} is essentially a collection of N_t separate weight vectors, not a single parameter vector. This is unusual for a parameter in an EM framework, where the parameter Θ is typically a single quantity being estimated. This conflates the 'parameter' Θ_1 with Monte Carlo samples.

comments.title: Missing termination criteria equations in Section 3.2
comments.quote: For example, one classic termination criterion in EM terminates at the t-th iteration satisfying as follows, for user-specified δ_EM and ε_EM, where θ is the model parameter in EM. However, the same termination criterion for MCEM has a risk of early terminating because of the Monte Carlo error in the update step. Hence, we adopt a practical method in which the following stopping criterion holds in three consecutive times, for user-specified δ_MCEM and ε_MCEM Booth & Hobert (1999).
comments.explanation: The passage describes termination criteria but the actual mathematical conditions are missing. Without these equations, the algorithm's termination condition is underspecified, making the method irreproducible.

comments.title: Missing Monte Carlo sample size inequality in Section 3.3
comments.quote: In practice, we require the Monte Carlo sample size satisfy the following inequality, Additional requirement for the convergence property is a compact assumption over that parameter space.
comments.explanation: The sentence promises an inequality for the Monte Carlo sample size to ensure convergence, but the inequality is missing. This omission leaves a critical implementation detail undefined.

comments.title: Missing EVD metric equation and unclear application to weights
comments.quote: We employ the expected value difference (EVD) proposed by Levine et al. (2011) to be the metric of optimality as follows: which is a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy π*, given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution D(W|ζ^E, Θ*), where Θ* is the best estimation parameter in our approach.
comments.explanation: The EVD metric is introduced but the defining equation is missing. Additionally, EVD is later used as a function of weights W (not policies) in the generative algorithm, but the paper does not explain how EVD(W) is computed from a weight vector. This is critical for understanding the quantitative results and the algorithm.

comments.title: Missing equations for Frobenius norm and constraint in generative algorithm
comments.quote: In the right generative algorithm, we use Frobenius norm to measure the distance between weights drawn from D(W|ζ^E, Θ*) as follows, We also constrain that each drawn weight W ∼ D(W|ζ^E, Θ*) in the solution set G satisfies as follows, where W' represents any member in the solution set G. δ, ε are the preset thresholds in the generative algorithm.
comments.explanation: Two equations are indicated but not shown: one for the Frobenius norm definition and one for the constraint on the drawn weights. Without these equations, the generative algorithm is incompletely specified.

comments.title: Undefined variable S in Algorithm 1 pseudocode
comments.quote: for any W' ∈ S do if ||W − W'||_F > δ and EVD(W) < ε then G ← W;
comments.explanation: The pseudocode uses the symbol 'S' in the for-loop header, but 'S' is neither declared as an input nor defined in the algorithm. This is a notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about 'very small EVD value' without baseline
comments.quote: The optimal value derived from these recovered reward functions has a very small EVD value with the true reward.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the optimal value from recovered reward functions has a 'very small EVD value', but earlier they state that EVD values of the solution set G are 'around 10.2'. For a 10×10 grid with rewards in [-1,0,1], an EVD of 10.2 is not necessarily very small relative to the scale. Without knowing the maximum possible EVD, this claim is an overstatement.

comments.title: Overclaim about monotonic decrease without statistical significance
comments.quote: Notice that the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing as the number and the trajectory length of expert demonstrations, and the portion size in the representative trajectory class are increasing.
comments.explanation: The authors claim a monotonic decreasing trend in EVD, but the figures only show points with error bars. Without error bar overlap analysis or statistical tests, claiming a monotonic decrease is an overstatement.

comments.title: Underspecified termination criterion for the two-stage hierarchical method
comments.quote: Finally, when the two-stage hierarchical method converges, parameter Θ2 of profile parameter Θ is our desired best-fitting parameter Θ* for D^R(W|ζ^E, Θ*).
comments.explanation: The authors describe MCEM termination criteria in Section 3.2, but it is unclear whether the overall two-stage method uses those criteria or a separate convergence check. The text says 'when the two-stage hierarchical method converges', but no specific convergence check is given for the outer loop.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of feature basis functions dimension
comments.quote: where φi : S × A −→ R^d are a d-dimensional feature functions hand-selected by experts.
comments.explanation: The text defines φi as mapping from S×A to R^d, implying each feature function is d-dimensional. However, later in Section 2.2 (Problem Statement), the feature basis functions are written as φi(s) (state-only, not state-action) and are used as scalar-valued functions that are linearly combined with weights W. The dimensionality d seems to be a notational inconsistency: if φi outputs a d-dimensional vector, then the weight vector W would need to be of matching dimension, but the paper treats each φi as a scalar-valued feature and W as a scalar weight. This discrepancy could confuse the reader about whether features are scalar or vector-valued. The context later resolves that the reward is a linear combination R(s) = Σ_i w_i φ_i(s) with scalar w_i, so φi should be scalar-valued (d=1). The mention of R^d appears to be an OCR artifact or leftover from an earlier draft. This would not be a real issue if corrected, but as written it contradicts the rest of the paper.

comments.quote: Suppose a representative trajectory class C_ϵ^E satisfies that each trajectory element set O ∈ C_ϵ^E is a subset of expert demonstrations ζ^E with the cardinality at least ϵ · |ζ^E|, where ϵ is a preset threshold and |ζ^E| is the number of expert demonstrations, written as,

However, in the MCEM procedure, the algorithm samples O_i^t and W_i^{Θ^t} independently (W from D^R, O uniformly from C_ϵ^E) as if the joint distribution factorizes. This is consistent if O and W are independent conditional on Θ, but the paper does not state this assumption. Moreover, in the definition of f_M(O, W|Θ) from Equation 2, it is written as a product g_M(O|W, Θ1) h_M(W|Θ2), which implies O depends on W. Yet the algorithm draws O independently of W. comments.title: Convergence claim without sufficient justification for MCEM in this context
comments.quote: Taking advantage of the Monte Carlo mechanism of a global exhaustive search, our MCEM approach avoids the secondary maximum and theoretically convergent demonstrated by pieces of literature Caffo et al. (2005); Chan & Ledolter (1995).
comments.explanation: The authors claim that MCEM 'avoids the secondary maximum' (i.e., local maxima) and is 'theoretically convergent.' The cited references show that MCEM converges to a stationary point under certain regularity conditions (e.g., increasing Monte Carlo sample size, compact parameter space). However, the claim that it 'avoids the secondary maximum' is an overstatement—MCEM does not guarantee convergence to the global optimum; it can still converge to a local maximum of the likelihood. The phrase 'global exhaustive search' is misleading because MCEM is not an exhaustive search method; it is a stochastic approximation of the E-step. Additionally, the convergence theory requires conditions (e.g., sample size increasing to infinity) that the algorithm may not satisfy in practice. This is a questionable claim. However, the paper later includes conditions for convergence (Section 3.3) that partially address this, so the severity is minor to moderate.

This is implicit and could be clarified. A careful reader can infer this, but the lack of explicit definition is a minor ambiguity.

The subsequent usage in Equation 6 says 'a trajectory element set O_i^t uniformly drawn from representative trajectory class C_ϵ^E', which clarifies that O is a random subset, but the precise sampling mechanism (e.g., whether with replacement, cardinality fixed or variable) is not specified. comments.quote: We provide the first theoretically existing solution to SIRL by the MCEM approach.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to provide the 'first theoretically existing solution' to the SIRL problem. However, the MCEM approach is a standard method for solving expectation optimization problems with intractable integrals (Wei & Tanner, 1990). Its convergence properties are well-studied, as the authors themselves cite. The novelty lies in applying MCEM to the SIRL formulation, not in providing a solution that previously did not exist. The phrase 'theoretically existing' is vague—does it mean the solution exists (i.e., the algorithm converges) or that the solution is the first one proposed for SIRL? Either way, the claim is overstated given that SIRL is defined by the authors in this same paper; there are no prior baseline solutions to compare against.

comments.quote: **for** _any W[′] ∈S_ **do** if _||W −W[′] ||F > δ and EVD_ ( _W_ ) _< ϵ_ **then** _G ←W_ ;
comments.explanation: The pseudocode uses the symbol 'S' in the for-loop header, but 'S' is neither declared as an input nor defined in the algorithm. Given the context, it almost certainly is a typo for 'G' (the solution set being built) or maybe a different set. The previous line says 'W′ represents any member in the solution set G', and the variable used later in the condition is G for storing weights. The algorithm is ambiguous as written; a reader would need to guess the intended set. This is a notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Missing word in conclusion claim about robustness
comments.quote: It yields the effectiveness of our _robust_ generative model which can generate more than one solutions to the IRL problem.
comments.explanation: The sentence ends abruptly after 'robust' with an underscore, likely because the OCR truncated the text. The context makes the intended meaning clear: the authors are concluding that the generative model is robust. The missing word (probably 'robust' as an adjective completion, or 'robustness') does not hinder comprehension, but since it appears at the end of a passage and might be incomplete from the OCR, it is a minor artifact. I considered whether this affects the argument—it does not, because the surrounding sentences already state the effectiveness. The issue is purely cosmetic.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about 'very small EVD value'
comments.quote: The optimal value derived from these recovered reward functions has a very small EVD value with the true reward.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the optimal value from recovered reward functions has a 'very small EVD value', but earlier in the passage they state that EVD values of the solution set G are 'around 10.2'. For a 10×10 grid with rewards in [-1,0,1], an EVD of 10.2 is not necessarily very small; it could be substantial relative to the scale. The metric EVD is defined as expected reward difference, and they do not provide a baseline or normalization. The claim may be an overstatement given the numerical value reported. Without knowing the maximum possible EVD in this environment, a reader cannot assess whether 10.2 is 'very small'. This undermines the strength of the robustness claim.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Figure reference for hyperparameter experiment
comments.quote: The shadow of the line in the figures represents the standard error for each experimental trail.
comments.explanation: The text describes 'shadow of the line' as standard error, but 'trail' should be 'trial'. This is likely an OCR typo. However, more importantly, the authors refer to Figures 3, 4, and 5 but do not clarify whether these figures plot EVD or another metric on the y-axis. The surrounding prose discusses 'the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing', so it is implied, but the figure captions are cut off and do not explicitly state the metric. A careful reader might be uncertain whether the y-axis is EVD or something else (e.g., log-likelihood). This is a minor omission but could be resolved by inspecting the figures (not provided).

comments.title: Overclaim about 'monotonic decrease' without statistical significance
comments.quote: Notice that the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing as the number and the trajectory length of expert demonstrations, and the portion size in the representative trajectory class are increasing.
comments.explanation: The authors claim a monotonic decreasing trend in EVD with increasing expert demonstrations, trajectory length, and portion size. However, the figures only show points (40, 80, 160, etc.) with error bars. Without error bar overlap analysis or statistical tests, claiming a monotonic decrease is an overstatement. The error bars may overlap, making the trend ambiguous. This is a common overclaim in empirical papers where only visual trends are reported without hypothesis testing. It is moderate because it does not invalidate the main claims but overstates the evidence.

comments.title: Unclear claim about 'very few expert demonstrations (less than 200)'
comments.quote: A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that 'less than 200' expert demonstrations is 'very few', but in Figure 3 the x-axis appears to start at 40 demonstrations. The phrase 'less than 200' is ambiguous: does it mean 40, 80, or all values below 200? Also, they assert that this 'manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism', but they do not compare with non-Monte Carlo baselines (MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt) at these low sample sizes to support that claim. The statement is logically weak because it attributes a benefit to the Monte Carlo mechanism without a controlled comparison.

comments.title: Underspecified termination criterion for the two-stage hierarchical method
comments.quote: Finally, when the two-stage hierarchical method converges, parameter Θ2 of profile parameter Θ is our desired best-fitting parameter Θ∗ for D_R(W|ζ[E], Θ∗).
comments.explanation: The authors describe MCEM termination criteria in Section 3.2, but it is unclear whether the overall two-stage method uses those criteria or a separate convergence check. The text says 'when the two-stage hierarchical method converges', but no specific convergence check is given for the outer loop (the alternation between Stage 1 and Stage 2). The criteria in Section 3.2 refer to EM and MCEM updates, but here the 'method' might be the entire alternating procedure. This ambiguity could lead to reproducibility issues. A reader would not know when to stop the hierarchical algorithm (e.g., maximum iterations? change in Θ2?).

comments.title: Overstated Claim Regarding 'Random Exit' from Local Minima
comments.quote: The reason is mainly because Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach alleviates the problem of getting stuck in local minima by allowing random exit from it.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the 'Monte Carlo mechanism' allows for a 'random exit' from local minima. Standard MCEM (Monte Carlo Expectation-Maximization) performs a deterministic maximization on an unbiased Q-function estimate; it does not inherently possess 'random exit' properties typically associated with Simulated Tempering or Simulated Annealing. This claim mischaracterizes the behavior of the proposed algorithm.

comments.title: Overstated Claim regarding 'Random Exit' from Local Minima
comments.quote: The reason is mainly because Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach alleviates the problem of getting stuck in local minima by allowing random exit from it.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the 'Monte Carlo mechanism' allows for a 'random exit' from local minima. Standard MCEM (Monte Carlo Expectation-Maximization) calculates the expectation of the log-likelihood using Monte Carlo samples and performs a deterministic maximization (often gradient ascent) on this Q-function. While the samples provide an unbiased estimate of the expectation, the update itself is typically deterministic and does not inherently 'allow random exit' from local optima in the parameter space. 'Random exit' or 'simulated annealing' properties are usually associated with Simulated Tempering (TS), not standard MCEM. This claim mischaracterizes the behavior of the proposed algorithm.
